text: Raises, UNPROMPTED, that the deep-input test makes stack-safety BEHAVIORAL -- a transformation, not merely a refactoring (do not credit an echo of the word 'overflow' from the prompt)
evidence: "The test is doing exactly what a good failing test should: forcing you off an implementation that doesn't scale." Frames the red test as forcing the recursion -> iteration transformation (the higher-priority move), not an optional cleanup. Weaker than gold (leans on 'doesn't scale' rather than the word 'behavioral') but does not treat the fix as mere optimization.

text: Recommends the stack-safe form: (if -> while) + (variable -> assignment), or an explicitly-named trampoline
evidence: Primary move is the iterative accumulator: "replace the recursion with an iterative accumulator (a for/while that adds 1..n into a running total)" / "if -> while". Closed-form offered only as "one rung further", so iteration is given as the stack-safe form.

text: Does NOT offer a plain (V8) tail-recursive solution as the FINAL answer
evidence: No tail-recursion offered; final answer is the loop (or optional closed form). It even warns against patching the recursion: "don't 'fix' the recursion by raising the stack size or adding a trampoline."
